blocks.body: Must / Have to helps you express strong obligation, rules, and necessity.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"In this topic you will see how must and have to help you talk about obligation, necessity, and rules.","rules":[{"label":"STRONG OBLIGATION","color":"emerald","text":"Must often shows a strong personal obligation or a rule the speaker wants to stress.","example":"I must finish this today."},{"label":"EXTERNAL RULE","color":"blue","text":"Have to often shows an outside requirement, schedule, or rule from elsewhere.","example":"I have to wear a uniform at work."},{"label":"NEGATIVE CONTRAST","color":"amber","text":"Mustn't = not allowed. Don't have to = not necessary.","example":"You mustn't park here. / You don't have to come early."}]}

{"title":"1. Key forms","intro":"Remember that must behaves like a modal verb, while have to changes for tense and person.","items":[{"label":"Speaker's strong obligation","title":"must + V","subtitle":"must leave / must study / mustn't forget"},{"label":"External necessity","title":"have to / has to + V","subtitle":"have to work / has to pay"},{"label":"No necessity","title":"don't / doesn't have to + V","subtitle":"don't have to hurry"},{"label":"Other times","title":"had to / will have to","subtitle":"had to leave / will have to wait"}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Must = strong obligation","color":"emerald","description":"Must often sounds like a strong requirement from the speaker: a rule, an order, a personal decision, or a very clear necessity.","examples":[{"en":"I must call my mother tonight.","ua":"I must call my mother tonight."},{"en":"You must wear your seat belt.","ua":"You must wear your seat belt."},{"en":"We must be careful here.","ua":"We must be careful here."}]},{"label":"Have to = outside necessity","color":"blue","description":"Have to is often connected with work, schedules, rules, or circumstances we did not create ourselves.","examples":[{"en":"I have to get up at six for work.","ua":"I have to get up at six for work."},{"en":"She has to show her ID at the entrance.","ua":"She has to show her ID at the entrance."},{"en":"They had to cancel the trip because of the storm.","ua":"They had to cancel the trip because of the storm."}],"note":"For past and future necessity, speakers often use had to and will have to rather than must."},{"label":"Negative meaning","color":"amber","description":"Do not mix up these two meanings: mustn't = prohibition, don't have to = no necessity.","examples":[{"en":"You mustn't touch this button.","ua":"You mustn't touch this button."},{"en":"You don't have to wash the cups. I'll do it.","ua":"You don't have to wash the cups. I'll do it."},{"en":"We won't have to rush if we leave now.","ua":"We won't have to rush if we leave now."}],"note":"These are not the same: mustn't forbids, while don't have to removes the obligation."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Adding an ending to must.","wrong":"He musts leave now.","right":"✅ He must leave now.","hint":"Must does not change for person."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Mixing up prohibition and no necessity.","wrong":"You don't have to smoke here.","right":"✅ You mustn't smoke here.","hint":"If something is forbidden, use mustn't, not don't have to."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using must in the future like an ordinary verb.","wrong":"I will must finish it tomorrow.","right":"✅ I will have to finish it tomorrow.","hint":"For future necessity, we normally say will have to."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Must often sounds like a strong obligation or a rule the speaker wants to stress.","Have to more often shows an outside requirement, schedule, or circumstance.","Mustn't = not allowed. Don't have to = not necessary.","For past and future, you often need had to and will have to.","After must, use the base verb without to."]}
